coredata.dc:title: NIR-responsive carbon dots for efficient photothermal cancer therapy at low power densities
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Bijiang
Yang, Dewen
Pan, Dengyu
Wang, Liang
Zheng, Fengfeng
Shen, Wenwen
Zhang, Chen
Li, Xiaokai
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with unique optical properties are a stable and highly biocompatible fluorescent material offering enormous application potential in optical imaging and photothermal cancer therapy (PTT). However, the low photothermal conversion efficiency and the high power density still impede the development of CDs in PTT. Herein, the nitrogen and oxygen co-doped CDs (N-O-CDs) with strong absorbance in the near infrared (NIR) region were prepared from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and N-containing polymer of branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) via a one-step molecular fusion route. The as-prepared black N-O-CDs with high photostability and superb biocompatibility can be utilized not only as a new fluorescence imaging agent but also as an excellent PTT agent in vivo. The structure, optical properties, bioimaging, photothermal effect in vitro, and therapeutic efficiency in vivo of the N-O-CDs are investigated. The low power density (0.8 W cm−2) and high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 38.3%) facilitate N-O-CDs to act as an ideal theranostic agent for fluorescence imaging and photothermal therapy in vitro and in vivo. This work highlights CDs as an excellent candidate for efficient cancer therapy.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal assay of N-O-CDs on cancer cells in vitro
In vivo fluorescence imaging of N-O-CDs
Photothermal effect of N-O-CDs in vivo

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11774216 ) and the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 16ZR1412100 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) HRTEM image of N-O-CDs (inset: lateral size distribution). (c) AFM image of N-O-CDs (insets: height profile along the white line and height distribution). (d) Raman spectrum of N-O-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of N-O-CDs. (f) Survey XPS spectrum, (g) High-resolution C 1s spectrum, (h) High-resolution N 1s spectrum, and (i) High-resolution O 1s spectrum of N-O-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Vis-NIR absorption spectrum of N-O-CDs (200 μg mL−1), showing a wide absorption band from 600 to 850 nm. (b) Absorption spectrum of N-O-CDs (200 μg mL−1) as a function of PEI concentration. The ratio of TNP and BPEI is varied from 1:1 to 1:16. (c) PL and excitation (PLE) spectra of N-O-CDs. (d) PL spectra of N-O-CDs excited at different wavelengths varied from 340 nm to 420 nm. (e) Photostability test under 3-h continuous radiation using a 100 W xenon lamp. (f) Dependence of PL intensity on pH values. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Confocal laser-scanning microscopy (CLSM) images on Hela cells incubated with N-O-CDs (100 μg mL−1) excited by 405 nm. (b). In vivo fluorescence imaging of the tumor site from a mouse intratumor injection with N-O-CDs at 0 h, 5 min, 1 h, and 3 h post-treatment. The color bars represent the fluorescence intensity. Red circles indicate the position of the implanted tumor. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Temperature elevation of pure water and N-O-CDs with various concentrations as a function of time under irradiation with an 808 nm NIR laser at a power density of 0.8 W cm−2. (b) Plot of temperature change (ΔT) over a period of 300 s versus different concentrations of N-O-CDs. (c) Temperature elevation of N-O-CD (200 μg mL−1) solutions under different powers as a function of time. (d) The photothermal effect of N-O-CD (200 μg mL−1) solution under illumination with an 808 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2). The laser was shut off after irradiation for 300 s. (e) Plot of cooling time versus negative natural logarithm of the temperature driving force obtained from the cooling period. The time constant τs for heat transfer of the system was determined to be 181.37 s. (f) Temperature variation of the N-O-CD solution over several on/off cycles of 808-nm laser irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a, b) In vitro cytotoxicity of Hela cells after receiving treatments with N-O-CDs with/without 5 min exposure to NIR irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Tumor growth curves of mice after receiving different treatments including Saline, N-O-CDs, Saline + NIR, N-O-CDs (100 μg mL−1) + NIR, and N-O-CDs (200 μg mL−1) + NIR (Power density: 0.8 W cm−2). (b) Monitoring curves of the body weight of mice over the whole treatment period. (c) Pictures of tumors obtained from mice on day 18 after receiving different treatments. (d) Representative photographs of tumor-bearing mice after NIR irradiation at different time points. (e) The images of H&E-stained slices of tumors at next day after treatment. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of the PTT power density and conversion efficiency between N-O-CDs and previously reported CDs.

head.ce:title: NIR-responsive carbon dots for efficient photothermal cancer therapy at low power densities

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physical Education College, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with unique optical properties are a stable and highly biocompatible fluorescent material offering enormous application potential in optical imaging and photothermal cancer therapy (PTT). However, the low photothermal conversion efficiency and the high power density still impede the development of CDs in PTT. Herein, the nitrogen and oxygen co-doped CDs (N-O-CDs) with strong absorbance in the near infrared (NIR) region were prepared from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and N-containing polymer of branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) via a one-step molecular fusion route. The as-prepared black N-O-CDs with high photostability and superb biocompatibility can be utilized not only as a new fluorescence imaging agent but also as an excellent PTT agent in vivo. The structure, optical properties, bioimaging, photothermal effect in vitro, and therapeutic efficiency in vivo of the N-O-CDs are investigated. The low power density (0.8 W cm−2) and high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 38.3%) facilitate N-O-CDs to act as an ideal theranostic agent for fluorescence imaging and photothermal therapy in vitro and in vivo. This work highlights CDs as an excellent candidate for efficient cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new cancer therapy method, photothermal cancer therapy (PTT) has received tremendous interests in nanomedicine. PTT typically employs near-infrared (NIR) light-absorbing agents that convert laser energy into heat to kill cancer cells, generating irreversible cellular damage and subsequent tumor destruction. Compared to chemotherapy, the therapeutic effects of PTT occur at the tumor sites, leading to low side effects, high specificity and minimal invasiveness. High-efficiency PTT agents should exhibit strong absorbance in the NIR region, high photothermal conversion efficiency, and high systemic safety. Nowadays, PTT agents have been derived from noble metal-based nanomaterials [1–5] and organic NIR chromophores [6–9]. These agents usually exhibit strong absorption in the NIR region and relatively high photothermal conversion efficiency. However, the potential long-term toxicity and nondegradable nature of noble metal-based nanomaterials with high costs severely impede their application in vivo. For example, the use of surfactants during gold nanorods synthesis has been shown to cause toxicity [10,11], and gold nanoshells encapsulated by PEG 5000 proved difficult to excrete from the body through renal clearance [12]. Additionally, the low photostability and poor hydrophilicity of conventional NIR dyes restrict their therapeutic applications.

NIR-responsive carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene oxide (GO), have been widely studied as potent candidates for PTT agents [13–16]. However, the poor water solubility and long-term toxicity of CNTs hindered their potential applications in biomedicine. Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging kind of zero-dimensional (0D) carbonaceous nanomaterial with distinctive optical properties, extremely small size, excellent biocompatibility, and chemical stability [17–19]. In recent years, CDs have attracted intensive research interest and shown potential applications in diverse areas, such as bioimaging [20–22], biosensing [23–25], medical diagnosis [26], cancer therapy [27–32], light emitting diodes [33,34], solar cells [35] and photocatalysis [36,37]. Compared with organic NIR dyes and noble metal-based nanomaterials, CDs are preferable in high photostability against photobleaching and remarkable biocompatibility. The extremely small size of CDs enables them to excrete from the body through renal clearance [38]. Therefore, CDs exhibit lower in vivo toxicity than GO-based materials. Moreover, functionalized CDs show superior water-solubility and higher stability in physiological media than GO sheets. These advantages could expand the use of CDs as a potential NIR absorber for PPT applications.

Recently, CDs have been reported to be used as a PTT agent [39–44]. For example, Jing et al. [39] utilized NIR dyes to prepare CDs for photothermal therapy under the irradiation of 808 nm laser at a power of 2 W cm−2. Similarly, Zhang et al. [42] used dopamine as carbon source to synthesize CDs via a convenient and facile hydrothermal process, but only photothermal evaluation in vitro using 808 nm laser (1.5 W cm−2) has been investigated. Obviously, the previously reported CDs for photothermal therapy still suffer from certain disadvantages, such as the need of a high power density of NIR lasers, which can damage bystander tissues and cause severe side effects. Therefore, it is desirable to synthesize NIR-absorbing CDs for photothermal cancer therapy with a low power density and a high photothermal conversion efficiency.

In this study, we synthesize the NIR-absorbing nitrogen and oxygen co-doped CDs (N-O-CDs) using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) as the precursors via a one-step molecular fusion route. Significantly, the N-O-CDs exhibit strong optical absorbance in the NIR region, due to heavy doping with pyrrole N, graphite N, and -OH. Therefore, they demonstrate high-efficiency heat generation under the irradiation of 808 nm laser at a low power density of 0.8 W cm−2, achieving an excellent therapeutic efficacy with 100% of tumor destruction. The tumor was ablated via photothermal therapy without bringing any side effects. To our best of knowledge, the laser power density of the CDs is lowest compared with those of previously reported CDs.

The N-O-CDs were synthesized by a one-step modified molecular fusion route using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and N-containing polymer of branched polyethylenimine (PEI molecular weight 10000) as the precursors. In a typical procedure, pyrene (4 g, TCI, purity > 98%) was nitrated into TNP in hot HNO3 (320 mL) at 80 °C under refluxing and stirring for 48 h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with deionized (DI) water and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the acid. The resultant TNP (40 mL) was mixed with BPEI (0.8 g) by ultrasonication for 1 h. The suspension was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 10 h. After cooled to room temperature, the N-O-CDs product was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon. The colloid was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for two days and dried at 80 °C for structural characterization.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were taken using a SPM-9600 AFM. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on FEI Tecnai F30 S-TWIN at 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550 under Cu Kα radiation. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature on a Hitachi 3100 spectrophotometer and a Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of dried samples were recorded using a Bio-Rad FTIR spectrometer FTS165. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λex = 633 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were collected using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Zeta potential was measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer.
QYs of the obtained N-O-CDs were determined by a relative method. Specially, Quinine Sulfate (QY = 55% in 0.1 M H2SO4) was selected as the reference for the emission range of 400–480 nm.

The QY of a sample was then calculated according to the following equation:
QY = QY ´ ×
F
F ´
×
A ´
A
×
n 2
n
´ 2
where QY is the quantum yields of the testing sample, F is the testing sample's integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index (1.33 for water), and A is the optical density. The superscript “´” refers to the referenced fluorescence dyes of known QYs. To obtain more reliable results, a series of solutions of N-O-CDs and referenced fluorescence dyes were prepared with concentrations adjusted such that the optical absorbance values were between 0 and 0.1 at 350 nm. The PL spectra were measured and the PL intensity was integrated. QYs were determined by comparison of the integrated PL intensity vs absorbance curves (refractive index, n, had also to be considered).

The N-O-CDs was diluted to various concentrations (0–200 μg mL−1), each solution was introduced in a quartz cuvette and irradiated with 808 nm laser at a power density of 0.8 W cm−2 for 5 min to measure the photothermal performance of N-O-CDs. And then irradiated with different laser power densities (0.4–1 W cm−2) to investigate the laser power dose effect. In order to observe the temperature change, a thermocouple probe was inserted into the quartz cuvette to contact the solution, and the temperature change was recorded every 20 s by a digital thermometer. Water was used as a control. The photostability of N-O-CDs was investigated by monitoring their absorbance changes under 808 nm laser irradiation at a power density of 0.8 W cm−2 for different time points.

Photothermal conversion efficiency was evaluated by recording the temperature change of the N-O-CD (200 μg mL−1) as a function of time under 808 nm laser irradiation at a power density of 0.8 W cm−2. Photothermal conversion efficiency, η, was calculated using Equation (1), as described by referring to the previous literature [45].
(1)
η =
h A
(
T
m a x
−
T
s u r r
)
−
Q
D i s
I
( 1 −
10
− A λ
)
Where h is the heat transfer coefficient, A is the surface area of the container. Tmax (55.4 °C) and Tsurr (23.2 °C) indicate the equilibrium temperature and the ambient temperature, respectively. QDis expresses the heat dissipation from the light absorbed by the quartz sample cell, which is 37.8 mW. I is incident laser power (0.8 W cm−2), and Aλ is the absorbance of N-O-CDs at 808 nm (0.14129). The value of hA can be calculated by equation (2).
(2)
τ s
=
m D
c D
h A
where τs is the sample system time constant, mD and cD are the mass (0.2 g) and heat capacity (4.2 J g−1) of deionized water used as a solvent, respectively. To calculated τs, a dimensionless parameter θ should be acquired by following equation (3).
(3)
θ =
Δ T
Δ
T
m a x
ΔT is the temperature change, a temperature difference between the solution temperature and ambient temperature. ΔTmax is the temperature change at the maximum steady-state temperature.

Thus, τ can be determined by applying the linear time data from the cooling period versus ln(θ) by following equation (4).
(4)
t = − τ
( ln
θ )
Therefore, hA can be calculated.

Hela cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle (DMEM) medium (Gibco, USA) with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Hyclone, USA). Approximately 2 × 105 Hela cells were seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and cultured using the same culture medium (2 mL per dish) at 37 °C under 5% CO2/95% air. All cells were incubated for at least 24 h until ∼80% confluence was reached. The N-O-CD aqueous solution was introduced to the cells with a final concentration of 100 μg mL−1 in the culture medium. The cells were examined under a confocal microscope (CarlZeiss LSM710, GER) using lasers of 405 nm.

Cell viability assays were performed using a MTT assay with 3-(4,5-dimethyl-thiazole-2-yl)-2,5-phenyltetrazolium bromide. The cell viability was expressed as the percentage of viable cells in total cells. In brief, these cells were respectively seeded in 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well) and allowed to adhere overnight. Then the medium was replaced by a fresh one containing the N-O-CDs at a dose of 12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 μg mL−1, respectively. With a further incubation for 24 h and 48 h, 20 μL of MTT was added and then incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Finally, 150 μL DMSO was added after the medium was removed and the absorbance of each well at 490 nm was measured using a Microplate Reader (Thermo, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal assay of N-O-CDs on cancer cells in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Hela cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well. After incubated in DMEM for 24 h, the mediums were treated with N-O-CDs of various concentration (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg mL−1) for 4 h. Then the cells were irradiated by 808 nm laser at a power density of 0.8 W cm−2 for 5 min. Afterwards, the cells were incubated for another 24 h or 48 h, a MTT assay was carried out to measure the cell viabilities.

Female Balb/c nude mice were provided by the SLAC Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All animal experiments were performed following “The National Regulation of China for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals” and approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanghai University. All experiments were performed in the logarithmic phase of cell growth. The Hela tumor models were established by subcutaneous injection of 5 × 106 cells in 100 μL serum-free DMEM medium into the axillary of nude mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging of N-O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In vivo fluorescence imaging was conducted using the IVIS Lumina III in vivo Imaging System (PerkinElmer, USA). A nude mouse was subcutaneously injected with N-O-CDs (100 μg mL−1, 100 μL) at tumor site after being anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 1% pentobarbital and imaged with 420 nm as the excitation wavelength and collected emission spectrum at 520 nm. The autofluorescence was removed by the spectrum unmixing software.

To observe the metabolic behavior of N-O-CDs, mice bearing Hela tumors were intravenously injected at a dose of 200 μg mL−1. The mice were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 1% pentobarbital and then imaged using the in vivo Imaging System at 10 min, 1, 2, 4, 5, 8, and 24 h. Major organs were collected for observation of their tissue distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effect of N-O-CDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the photothermal efficacy of N-O-CDs in vivo, the mice were randomly divided into five groups when the tumor volume reached approximately 100 mm3 (five mice per group): (a) saline control, (b) N-O-CDs (200 μg mL−1) without NIR laser, (c) irradiated with NIR laser (808 nm, 0.8 W cm−2, 5 min), (d) intratumorally injected with N-O-CD solution (100 μg mL−1, 100 μL) and irradiated under NIR laser, (e) intratumorally injected with N-O-CD solution (200 μg mL−1, 100 μL) and irradiated under NIR laser. The temperature changes of tumor site were monitored by a Fluke infrared thermal imaging camera. After treatments, tumor sizes were measured every other day and mice body weight was measured every day. The tumor volume was determined according to the following equation: V= (A × B2)/2, where A and B are the maximum and minimum diameter of the tumor, respectively.

12 Healthy female Balb/c mice were intravenously injected with N-O-CDs (200 μg mL−1, 200 μL) and sacrificed at 1, 7 and 21 days (4 mice per time point). Other 12 Balb/c mice were used as control groups and sacrificed at 1, 7 and 21 days p.i. (4 mice per time points). Major organs from the treated mice were collected for hematoxylin & eosin (H&E) staining by the standard protocol and examined by a digital microscope (Leica QWin), which were conducted by Wuhan Saiweier Biological Technology Co., Ltd.

All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Statistical differences were analyzed using a Student's t-test. * denotes a statistical significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001) between the experimental data of two groups.

We reported a one-step hydrothermal molecular fusion route to synthesize the N-O-CDs by simply controlling the fusion of TNP and the hydrolysis of BPEI at 220 °C for 10 h (Fig. 1 ). The TNP precursor was obtained from the nitration of pyrene according to our previous work [46]. The detailed synthetic procedure was described in the experimental section. After the hydrothermal reaction, the crude product was dialyzed against water for two days to remove unreacted molecules and other impurities. The as-prepared N-O-CDs can easily dissolve in DI water to form a clear black solution under natural light (the left in Fig. S1). Moreover, the colloidal N-O-CDs in solution were stable without any precipitate formed for more than three months. The colloidal stability of the N-O-CDs in serum is especially important for photothermal therapy. After the N-O-CDs (200 μg mL−1) mixed with PBS and fetal bovine serum (FBS), the N-O-CDs kept stable for 30 days (Fig. S2). Compared to the control group, no significant precipitate was formed.

We utilized transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) to examine the morphologies of the N-O-CDs. As revealed by TEM, N-O-CDs had a narrow size distribution in the range of 1.2–3.3 nm (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2b) shows that N-O-CDs exhibited crystalline lattice fringes with a (100) spacing of 0.21 nm [47]. Their average thickness determined by AFM is 1.06 nm, corresponding to layer number of 1–2 (Fig. 2c) [48]. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-O-CDs is characteristic of a graphite-like structure with a broader (002) layer spacing of 4.41 Å (Fig. S3). Due to the hydroxyl functionalization at edge sites, the interlayer spacing is slightly larger than the (002) plane spacing of the graphite (3.34 Å). Raman spectroscopy is a useful tool for the structural characterization of the N-O-CDs. Two characteristic G and D bands of N-O-CDs were observed at 1584 and 1379 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2d). The G band arises from plane vibrations of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, and the D band is related to topological defects or sp3-hybridized carbon atoms [49]. The ordered G band is stronger than the disordered D band with a large G to D intensity ratio of 1.09, reflecting the good crystalline quality of the N-O-CDs.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the N-O-CDs (Fig. 2e) clearly shows the stretching vibration peak of -OH groups at 3365 cm−1 [50]. There are also two strong stretching vibration peaks at 1437 and 1662 cm−1, ascribed to C-N and N-H bonds, respectively, indicating that nitrogen was doped in the N-O-CDs. We further performed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements to determine the doping content and chemical state of nitrogen and oxygen in the N-O-CDs. The full XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 2f shows three typical peaks: C 1s (284 eV), N 1s (399 eV), and O 1s (531 eV), giving contents of 67.79 at. % (C),19.09 at. % (N), and 13.12 at. % (O). The N/C and O/C atomic ratios are 28.16% and 19.35%, respectively, indicating that N-O-CDs were highly doped with N and O. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2g), the band can be deconvoluted into three peaks, corresponding to C-C/C=C (285.1 eV), C−N (286.5 eV), and C−OH (287.5 eV). The N 1s band can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 398.8 and 400.7 eV, representing pyrrole N (94.53 at. %) and graphitic N (5.47 at. %), respectively (Fig. 2h). The positive charge (+16.7 mV) of N-O-CDs is due to the pyrrole N at the edges (Fig. S4). The O 1s band contains a single peak of O-H at 530.9 eV (Fig. 2i). The functionalization of N-O-CDs with these groups (OH, pyrrole N, and pyrrole N) is shown in Fig. 1.

The Vis-NIR absorption (Abs), photoluminescence (PL), and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the N-O-CDs in aqueous solution were shown in Fig. 3 . The Abs spectrum reveals that N-O-CDs have a broad range of optical absorption (600–850 nm) from visible light to NIR (Fig. 3a). Recently, several studies have shown that the absorption and emission peaks of CDs or CDs shift to longer wavelengths as the nitrogen content increases [51–55]. The influence of different types of nitrogen centers on the optical properties of N-doped carbon dots (CDs) was systematically studied by Sarkar et al. The results showed that nitrogen species including pyridinic, pyrrolic, and amino centers have no appreciable effects on the CDs' absorption properties, but graphitic nitrogen dopants can inject excess electrons into the unoccupied π* orbitals and significantly reduce the HOMO−LUMO gap and the energies of the corresponding optical transitions, leading to the marked red-shift in their UV-vis absorption spectrum [56–57]. Simultaneously, the surface O-containing functional group of the CDs consequently narrows the energy levels and causes the red-shifted absorption [58–60]. Therefore, the strong NIR-absorption of N-O-CDs is ascribed to the heavily N-doped and O-containing functional groups at the edges. In order to investigate the effects of N doping content on the Abs spectrum in NIR region, we obtained N-O-CDs with different N contents by changing the ratio of TNP to BPEI. The Abs spectra of N-O-CDs with the same concentration (200 μg mL−1) as a function of BPEI concentration is shown in Fig. 3b. When the ratio of TNP to PEI is 1:8, the absorption of N-O-CDs at 808 nm is the strongest.

The PL quantum yields (QYs) of the N-O-CDs were determined to be 16.1% using Quinine Sulfate as the reference (Fig. S5). Fig. 3c shows that the PL peak of N-O-CDs at 475 nm can be excited in a wide wavelength range. The main PLE bands (at 380 nm) are well consistent with the corresponding excitonic absorption bands (375 nm), suggesting that the emission is characterized by band-edge exciton-state decay rather than defect-state decay [46]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the PL peak barely shifts with the excitation wavelength changed from 340 to 420 nm (with 20 nm interval), indicating that the prepared N-O-CDs did not present the excitation-dependent PL behavior as previously reported GQDs or CDs [26]. This may be attributed to the high crystallinity of N-O-CDs with few defects. Additionally, the N-O-CDs emit bright cyan fluorescence under the irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp (the right in Fig. S1). The fluorescence stability of the N-O-CDs in aqueous solution was also tested under 3-h continuous radiation using a 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 3e, the PL intensity of N-O-CDs slightly increased rather than reduced under a long UV exposure for 3 h. This result demonstrated that N-O-CDs are highly photostable with no photobleaching effect observed. The N-O-CDs also exhibit stable PL against the variation of pH from 2 to 10 (Fig. 3f).

We investigated the fluorescence imaging ability in vitro and the cellular uptake behavior of N-O-CDs, using confocal laser-scanning microscopy (CLSM). The CLSM images were obtained under the excitation of 405 nm laser with the detection wavelength in the 420–480 nm range (Fig. 4a). After incubating Hela cells with N-O-CDs (100 μg mL−1) for 10 min at 37 °C, a bright fluorescence signal was observed in the cytoplasm, indicating that the N-O-CDs were internalized by the cells. What's more, there was no apparent fluorescence intensity change after a continuous exposure to 405 nm laser for 30 min, revealing the excellent photostability of the N-O-CDs suitable for potential long-term cell imaging (Fig. S6). Meanwhile, the rapid cellular uptake of N-O-CDs was beneficial for improving the phototherapeutic efficiency.

The in vivo toxicity of the N-O-CDs was evaluated by H&E-stained slices of major organs after intravenous injection of N-O-CDs (200 μg mL−1, 200 μL). As shown in Fig. S7, there were no obvious differences between control and treatment groups, confirming that N-O-CDs have no obvious toxicity in vivo. Because of their high safety, the feasibility for in vivo fluorescent imaging was investigated by using Hela tumor-bearing Balb/c nude mice as the animal model. Mice were intratumorally injected with 100 μL of N-O-CDs at 100 μg mL−1, and five min later, the tumors were imaged with the 520 nm fluorescence from N-O-CDs excited at 420 nm. A strong fluorescence signal from the N-O-CDs was observed in the tumor area (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence intensity became increasingly strong, indicating an increasing cellular uptake of N-O-CDs in the tumor region. The fluorescence signal became the strongest at 1 h, and tended to steadily decrease over time (Fig. S8).

We further investigated the metabolic behavior of N-O-CDs in Hela tumor-bearing nude mice via in vivo fluorescence imaging. Mice were intravenously injected with 200 μL of N-O-CDs (200 μg mL−1) before anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 1% pentobarbital. In vivo fluorescence images were then collected at 10 min, 1, 2, 4, 5, 8, and 24 h, respectively. At 1 and 5 h post-injection, bright fluorescence was observed in the area of bladder (Fig. S11a). As the extension of time, the fluorescence intensity was gradually reduced. No meaningful fluorescence was detected in the area of bladder after 8 h. The result indicates that the N-O-CDs can be rapidly excreted from the mice.

Ex vivo fluorescence images of organs (liver and kidney) excised from mice (sacrificed at 0, 2, 4, and 24 h post-injection) were also taken to observe the excretion pathway of the N-O-CDs. N-O-CDs accumulated in the liver and kidney (Fig. S11c). Moreover, the intensity of the fluorescence signals gradually decreased with time, which was consistent with the observation by in vivo imaging (Fig. S11b). The faeces at different time points after i.v. injection were examined by UV-vis absorption and PL spectra shown in Fig. S12. The fluorescence signal intensity in faeces of 4 h treated group was significantly stronger than that of other treated groups, while no meaningful fluorescence signal was observed in 24 h treated group. The above data suggested that our N-O-CDs could be rapidly excreted from hepatobiliary system and kidney system. The rapid renal excretion behavior from mice was also observed in many previously reported CQDs and GQDs [61,62], due to their lateral sizes smaller than the renal filtration threshold (∼5.5 nm) [63].

Based on the enhanced NIR absorption ability of N-O-CDs, we further examined their photothermal conversion effects under an 808 nm NIR laser irradiation. The temperature change as a function of time was recorded for different concentrations of the NIR absorber (12.5–200 μg mL−1) under the same laser power (0.8 W cm−2) (Fig. 5a). With the concentration increasing, the elevated temperature (relative to room temperature) of the N-O-CD solution under the same irradiation increased from 5.7 °C at 12.5 μg mL−1 to 32.2 °C at 200 μg mL−1 (Fig. 5b). The result indicates that N-O-CDs at a rather low concentration (200 μg mL−1) can effectively convert the NIR laser energy into heat. The photothermal performances of N-O-CDs were also investigated under varied laser power densities. As shown in Fig. 5c, the N-O-CD solution (200 μg mL−1) was exposed to the 808 nm laser for 5 min at power densities varied from 0.4 to 1 W cm−2. After the irradiation, the temperature of the irradiated solution reached 44.7 and 61.8 °C at a power density of 0.4 and 1 W cm−2, respectively, exhibiting a remarkable power dependent behavior: the temperature dramatically increased with slightly increasing the laser power density. According to these results, we can select the optimum irradiation conditions at a very low power density to derive enough heat (55.4 °C) to destroy tumor cells: 200 μg mL−1, 0.8 W cm−2, 5 min.

To measure the photothermal conversion efficiency of N-O-CDs, the temperature changes of N-O-CD solution (200 μg mL−1, 1 mL) as a function of time were recorded under irradiation of 808 nm laser (0.8 W cm−2). The Laser was shut off when the temperature reached steady state (Fig. 5d). On the basis of obtained data (Fig. 5e), the photothermal conversion efficiency can reach 38.3%, which is higher than that of previously reported CDs for PTT under the same conditions (Table 1 ) [39–44]. Moreover, the photostability of N-O-CDs was examined by recording their maximum absorbance changes under irradiation at a power density of 0.8 W cm−2 for different periods. As shown in Fig. 5f, the N-O-CDs have no obvious temperature decay after several on/off cycles of 808-nm laser irradiation, indicating that N-O-CDs have high photostability.

Based on the high photothermal conversion efficiency of N-O-CDs, MTT assay was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity and therapeutic effect in vitro on cancer cells. After incubating with different concentrations (12.5–200 μg mL−1) of N-O-CD solution for 4 h, the Hela cells were exposed to an NIR laser (808 nm, 0.8 W cm−2) for 5 min. Then, the cells were incubated for another 24 h or 48 h. As shown in Fig. 6 b, the viability of Hela cells decreased significantly as the concentration of N-O-CDs increased. Significantly, the majority of the cells were ablated at a concentration of 200 μg mL−1, with only about 13% of cells alive after 48 h incubation. In contrast, Hela cells exhibited high viability after 24 or 48 h incubation in the absence of laser irradiation (Fig. 6a). These results demonstrate that N-O-CDs hold great potential as a PTT agent for cancer therapy.

Motivated by outstanding photothermal effect of N-O-CDs in vitro, we further evaluated the tumor therapeutic effect in vivo. Hela cells were subcutaneously injected into the right axillary of female Balb/c nude mice to establish a tumor model. When the tumor volume reached approximately 100 mm3, mice were divided into five groups (five mice per group): saline, N-O-CDs, NIR laser only, N-O-CD solution (100 μg mL−1) + NIR laser, N-O-CD solution (200 μg mL−1) + NIR laser. Mice were intratumorally injected with N-O-CD solution and then the tumors were irradiated under an 808 nm laser at the power density of 0.8 W cm−2 for 5 min. The temperature change of the tumor areas were monitored through an infrared thermal camera (Fig. S10). According to the thermal images, upon the laser irradiation, the tumor temperature rapidly rose from 35.8 to 57.4 °C within 5 min in the presence of the CDs, while the temperature of the tumor injected with saline only increased to 42 °C under the same irradiation conditions. Such temperature changes were consistent with the in vitro thermal images upon the NIR laser irradiation (Fig. S9). To evaluate the therapeutic effect, tumor sizes were measured every other day and mice body weight was measured every day. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the tumors in the saline group, N-O-CDs without NIR laser group and the only NIR laser group grow rapidly. The tumors in the low-concentration N-O-CDs group were effectively suppressed upon laser irradiation. Subsequently, tumors in the N-O-CDs group with a high concentration (200 μg mL−1) showed that the tumors were completely ablated after the treatment, exhibiting an excellent PTT effect. As shown in Fig. 7d, scars formed at the tumor after the NIR laser irradiation for the next day and fell off 14 days later, indicating complete tumor ablation by PTT (Fig. 7c). For N-O-CD + NIR laser group, the tumor elimination rate was as high as 100%. During the whole treatment period, the weight of mice did not show obvious difference for all the groups (Fig. 7b). To further evaluate the therapeutic effect of N-O-CDs, mice were sacrificed at the next day after treatment. Then, tumors were collected for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. The images of H&E-stained slices of tumors exhibited tumor necrosis for the N-O-CDs + NIR laser group. In contrast, for the saline group and the only laser group, there was no tumor necrosis (Fig. 7e).

In summary, we have developed a high-efficiency PTT agent from biocompatible N-O-CDs with an excellent optical response to NIR lasers. The N-O-CDs exhibited a higher photothermal conversion efficiency of 38.3% under NIR irradiation. At low enough laser power (0.8 W cm−2), they achieved an excellent therapeutic efficacy with 100% of tumor destruction, while normal tissues did not be injured under the irradiation. The excellent PTT effect was ascribed to the high N and O doping, which can extend their absorption into the NIR region. Also, the N-O-CDs showed bioimaging ability in vitro and in vivo. These results show that N-O-CDs can hold promise in bioimaging and PTT applications. They would have potential in combination with immunotherapy [64] if their pharmacokinetics could be improved.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11774216) and the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 16ZR1412100).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.03.084.
